reasoning: Strong optimization opportunity exists. GenDB is 6-16x slower than DuckDB with clear, actionable bottlenecks. The Learner identified low-risk, high-impact optimizations: Q3 bloom filter could save 800-1200ms (37-55% of Q3 time), top-K heap saves 200-400ms, and Q1 perfect hashing saves 400-600ms. Combined impact could reduce total query time from 3645ms to ~1500-2000ms (2-2.4x speedup). With 2 iterations remaining, we can afford to take this optimization step and still have room for refinement.
focus_areas: Q3 top-K min-heap for LIMIT 10 (replace full sort with incremental tracking)
Q3 bloom filter on qualifying_orders (eliminate 88% of hash probes)
Q1 perfect hash aggregation (direct array indexing for 4 groups)
strategy_notes: Focus on operator-level optimizations before storage changes. Q3 is the primary bottleneck (60% of total time, 16x slower than DuckDB). Bloom filter + top-K heap should reduce Q3 from 2178ms to ~600-900ms. Q1 perfect hashing is a quick win (4 groups → array indexing). Skip storage changes (sorting, zone maps) for now - they require longer iteration cycles and are better suited for iteration 2-3 if needed. Prefer incremental, composable optimizations that can be validated independently.
notes: Implementation priorities: (1) Q3 top-K heap is simplest - replace aggregation map + sort with priority_queue, (2) Q1 perfect hashing - replace unordered_map with fixed array[9], (3) Q3 bloom filter - implement BloomFilter class (512KB, 3 hash functions) and filter lineitem before hash probe. All three are low-risk and additive. Expected total query time after optimization: ~1500-2000ms (vs current 3645ms).
